screens.name: Lead-sheet-team runtime contract
screens.purpose: Define the workflow structure, role boundaries, and state contract for a non-UI extraction team.

ux_rules: Keep lead-sheet-team clearly separate from the existing development AI team in naming, directories, and commands.
Model the workflow around extraction accuracy and traceability rather than design/build concerns.
Use explicit phase names that match the extraction pipeline: intake, preprocess, extract, align, verify, review, publish.
Prefer inspectable JSON artifacts and plain-text role prompts over opaque orchestration.
Make command naming symmetrical with the existing supervisor so operators can learn the new runtime quickly.

screen_contexts.layout_intent: Provide a compact, inspectable runtime surface expressed through files and commands rather than a visual app screen.
interaction_risks: Operators may confuse the new runtime with the development AI team if paths or commands overlap.
State contracts may accidentally drift back toward development-centric phases.
notes: This task is architecture scaffolding rather than end-user UI work.
Documentation and command symmetry carry most of the usability burden.

constraints: Do not modify the existing scripts/ai-team runtime while introducing lead-sheet-team.
Use a separate root state directory for lead-sheet-team.
Keep the first version lightweight and file-oriented, consistent with the current AI team MVP style.
open_decisions: Future database wiring between lead-sheet-team and the product app is deferred.
Future evidence assets and extraction tools are deferred until the team begins handling real score ingestion.

critical_judgments: The new runtime must be clearly independent from the existing development supervisor.
The phase loop must reflect lead-sheet extraction work rather than generic software delivery.
Role prompts and schemas must discourage inference and encourage explicit uncertainty.

visual_constraints: No new product UI is required for this step.
Usability should come from naming, documentation, and contract clarity.
